purpose: Explains the fundamental determinant of stock-bond correlation sign based on whether demand or supply shocks dominate, mediated by the central bank reaction function and the Phillips Curve slope.
components: Demand shock identification: growth/financial stress events → CB eases → bonds rally as equities fall → negative correlation
Supply shock identification: inflation/cost-push events → CB tightens → bonds and equities fall together → positive correlation
Central bank reaction function (Taylor Rule): converts shock type into policy response via inflation coefficient φ_π
Phillips Curve slope (0.1-0.5): determines how aggressively the CB must respond to supply shocks
Inflation expectations anchoring: determines whether CB can look through supply shocks without tightening
current_assessment: Universally accepted as the theoretical foundation by all 8 agents. Current ambiguity arises from mixed shock environment. The effective Taylor Rule coefficient (~1.0-1.2) is lower than textbook calibrations (1.5-2.0), making the correlation sign more shock-dependent. No agent contested the mechanism; all debate concerns parameterization and which shock dominates.
limitations: Does not account for fiscal dominance as a separate channel — requires Bianchi-Melosi extension
Assumes monetary transmission works as specified, which is partially broken (asset price channel counterproductive)
Phillips Curve slope parameter (0.1-0.5) is unresolved and conditions all predictions
Does not distinguish maturity-dependent correlation patterns without extension
Challenger_01 argues the framework produces unfalsifiable epicycles when applied to ambiguous shock mixes
